data.title: Walgreens drops as analysts skeptical over potential sale
data.description:
Walgreens Boots (NASDAQ:WBA) fell 7.3% as investors and analysts were skeptical about a potential sale of the drugstore chain to private equity firm Sycamore Partners

Shares of Walgreens (WBA) reversed court on Wednesday after surging 18% on Tuesday after the WSJ reported that Walgreens and Sycamore have been discussing a deal that could be completed early next year.

"While we acknowledge the context around a potential sale in a challenging pharmacy backdrop, a buyout is harder to contemplate given its already sizeable debt burden and paltry cash flow, making the value creation pathway harder to decipher," Morgan Stanley analyst Erin Wright, wrote in a note on Wednesday.

Wright highlighted Walgreen's (WBA) $9.5 billion of outstanding debt, ~$23 billion of operating leasing and ~$7 billion liability associated with opioid-related claims that could prove an impediment to a possible deal.

"We also see few logical strategic suitors in the wings amidst aforementioned pressures across both front end and pharmacy," Wright, who has an underweight rating and $7 price target on WBA wrote.

Raymond James analyst John Ransom also had issues with the math of a potential Walgreens (WBA) deal.

"We were surprised by this headline as we struggle to see how such a deal could get financed given WBA’s deteriorating earnings/cash flow outlook and ~$15B of liabilities on the balance sheet ($8.2B of net debt + $6.6B of opioid liabilities) along with other liabilities (~$2.1B for store closure costs) and a massive working capital deficit (~$8B)," Ransom, who has a market perform on WBA wrote in a note on Wednesday.

data.title: Apple working with Broadcom to develop in-house AI server chips: report
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) is working with Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) to develop the company's first in-house server chip exclusively designed for artificial intelligence, The Information reported.

The chip, known internally as Baltra, could be ready for mass production by 2026, the news outlet reported, citing people with direct knowledge of the matter. The new AI chip could be used for inferencing, which has the chip capable of processing new data and then hands them off to the large language models to produce an output, a task different than training models, the news outlet added.

The chip will be produced by Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) and likely use its 3 nanometer process, The Information added, citing sources.

Broadcom shares rose 6.5% in late trading, while Apple shares slipped fractionally. Nvidia (NVDA), which dominates the AI accelerator market, rose 3.5%.

Julian Lin, Investing Group Leader for Best Of Breed Growth Stocks, said the potential partnership is likely to benefit Broadcom more than Apple, as "the latter has historically in-sourced a lot of its technology."

"We might be seeing the predictable next stage of the AI hype cycle: high margins always attracts competition," Lin added.

The plan to bring its own chips to its own servers was born three years ago, but the plan was accelerated after OpenAI's ChatGPT chatbot took the world by storm in December 2022, according to previous reports.

Chief Executive Tim Cook has said previously Apple has some advantages that its competitors in the AI space do not. "We believe in the transformative power and promise of AI, and we believe we have advantages that will differentiate us in this new era, including Apple's unique combination of seamless hardware, software and services integration, groundbreaking Apple's silicon, with our industry-leading neural engines and our unwavering focus on privacy, which underpins everything we create," Cook said on the company's earnings call in May.

Last week, Apple executive Benoit Dupin gave a rare endorsement of Amazon's (AMZN) custom artificial intelligence chips and said the iPhone maker is evaluating Amazon's latest AI chip for pre-training models.

Cupertino, Calif.-based Apple has also used some of Google's (GOOG) (GOOGL) chips to train AI models for Apple Intelligence.

The news comes as Apple continued to roll out its Apple Intelligence features on Wednesday. The tech giant released the latest versions of iOS, macOS and iPadOS, all of which include several AI-related features, including the integration of ChatGPT into Siri.

Apple and Broadcom did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

(This story has been updated to include analyst commentary in the fourth and fifth paragraphs.)

data.title: The New York Times reaches tentative deal with tech workers union
data.description:
The News Guild of New York, which represents the Times Tech Guild, has reached a tentative agreement with The New York Times (NYSE:NYT) on a three-year contract, a month after the employees’ election week strike, the union said on Wednesday.

The tentative agreement is the first for the Times Tech Guild, which is a bargaining unit of The NewsGuild of New York, which represents nearly 6,000 media workers including two other units at The New York Times.

Some of the highlights of the tentative contract include enhanced job security, guaranteed wage increases for the first time of up to 8.25%, and additional compensation for on-call work, among others.

"We're thrilled to reach an agreement that secures just cause' protections, sustains workplace flexibility, and guarantees annual raises for our members," said Kathy Zhang, a Senior Analytics Manager at The New York Times and unit chair of the Tech Guild.

The Times Tech Guild had voted in September to authorize a strike to walk off the job around Election Day when there is usually higher readership. The union’s more than 600 members maintain the back-end systems running The Times’s digital operation.

The Tech Guild members will vote on ratification of their contract on Thursday, Dec. 19th.

data.title: Red Bull may have won the 2024 energy drink battle as it fended off Celsius and Monster
data.description:
Energy drink sales accelerated to 7.7% year-over-year for a four-week period of retail sales tracked by Nielsen that ended on November 30. Monster Beverage (NASDAQ:MNST) saw energy drink sales increase 3.6% Y/Y, while Celsius Holdings (NASDAQ:CELH) saw its sales growth slow to 5.9% and its market share retreat back to 8.8% after being as high as 9.7% in September.

Notably, Monster Beverage (MNST) saw a price/mix increase of 5.2% during the tracked period to offset a 1.6% drop in volume, while Celsius Holdings (CELH) saw its 10.1% volume growth help to offset a 4.1% decline in price/mix.

Weighing in on the energy drink market, Evercore ISI analyst Robert Ottenstein said 2024 has to be considered the year of Red Bull. He highlighted that Red Bull’s dollar market share fell to 35.0% in 2023 from 36.9% in 2022, with most of the share losses going to Celsius, which saw its own share leap from 5.3% to 10.1%. However, Red Bull was noted to have fought back hard this year with its Red Edition and Amber Edition sugar-free innovations, which ramped in June and then the Winter Edition sugar-free in October. "These line extensions not only blunted Celsius' growth, but also bit into Monster, which experienced share losses in 2023 and again in 2024 YTD," maintained Ottenstein.

Shares of Celsius Holdings (CELH) have skyrocketed over a five-year period, but got ahead of themselves in early 2024 as investors bet on the company to see even higher sales growth and market share.

Those expectations were impacted when distribution partner PepsiCo (PEP) reduced its orders to optimize its own inventory level. In addition, the energy drink market faced heightened competition from brands such as Keurig Dr Pepper's (KDP) Ghost Energy, Zoa Energy, and C4 Energy.

Monster Beverage (MNST) and Celsius Holdings (CELH) were both down about 1% in early afternoon trading on Wednesday.

data.title: Trump 2.0 will worsen the economic slowdown says Pantheon Macroeconomics
data.description:
President-elect Trump’s proposed increase in tariffs and deportation could worsen the slowdown triggered by higher interest rates, according to Pantheon Macroeconomics’ ‘The United States in 2025’ report.

Pantheon economists expect GDP and employment growth to continue to slow next year, pushing the FOMC to lower the funds rate further, despite stalling progress in core PCE inflation reduction.

“A post-election bounce in economic activity is unlikely, as private sector spending was unscathed by political uncertainty before the elections, and confidence has changed little since then,” wrote Samuel Tombs, head of the U.S. economic team at Pantheon, in the report. “Measures of consumers’ confidence have risen less than after Mr. Trump’s win in 2016, when higher sentiment failed to boost spending.”

In addition, he said that the Fed’s interest rate cuts have “done little” to alleviate pressures on the private sector balance sheets.

“The average investment-grade corporate bond yield is 75 basis points above the average coupon rate and roughly 150 basis points higher than yields in the late 2010s,” he said. “Interest payments for corporates, therefore, will continue to rise as existing debt is rolled over. Meanwhile, the average interest rate on short-term loans to small businesses was a stifling 8.8% in November, according to the NFIB survey.”

Also, households’ net interest income ae expected to continue to fall in 2025, since the effective interest rate on savings is sensitive to the federal funds rate – more so than the mortgage rate, which rose to 4.2% in the second quarter from 4% a year ago.

Firms will continue to suffer with high borrowing costs with expansion plans delays and squeezed costs, Tombs added, and said he expects payroll growth to average about 100,000 in the first half of next year, below the current rate of about 175,000. “This will keep the unemployment rate on its gently rising path, bearing down further on wage growth.”

Before the election, Pantheon economists expected the core CPE inflation to return to the 2% target by mid-2025, “but Mr. Trump looks set to throw a wrench into the works.”

Now, they are factoring a 0.5-1 percentage point uplist to the forecast in 2025 due to tariffs, Tombs said.

In addition, undocumented migrant deportations could boost core PCE inflation “by creating labor shortages and fueling strong wage increases in the manufacturing, construction and agriculture sectors.”

“Tougher controls on immigration will restrict labor supply in time, but inflows likely will surge prior to their implementation, buying the economy some time.”

data.title: Meta informs users of outage across its apps through X
data.description:
Mark Zuckerberg's Meta (NASDAQ:META) on Wednesday posted through its official account on Elon Musk's X that all of its social media apps, namely Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Threads, were down in what appears to be a global outage.

"We’re aware that a technical issue is impacting some users’ ability to access our apps. We’re working to get things back to normal as quickly as possible and apologize for any inconvenience," Meta said on X.

According to data from Downdetector, reports of Facebook outages reached 105k soon after 1:00 pm ET in the U.S. For Instagram, at least 69k reports were received.

The outages across Meta's apps are also being reported in major markets like India, Brazil, and the UK, according to Downdetector.

Shares of META were +2% by 2:30 pm ET on the Nasdaq.

data.title: Make that a double: Diageo gains a bull at UBS
data.description:
Thanks to strong growth momentum behind Don Julio and Crown Royal, Diageo’s (NYSE:DEO) business is towards the end of its earnings downgrade cycle, earning a double upgrade at UBS to Buy from Sell with a 27% hike to its price target to £29.20.

The sell-out trends in the U.S. continue to outperform a still weak spirits industry, with the growth in Don Julio and Crown Royal more than offsetting weakness in the rest of the portfolio. While destocking might persist which could drive down U.S. spirits shipments in the first half of 2025, the strong growth of Guinness in the on-trade channel results in an organic sales forecast of +0.6% in H1 versus the consensus estimate of -2.4%.

“There could be some upside risks to our North American forecast if sell-out trends remain robust into December,” UBS analysts Sanjeet Auila and Andrei Condrea said in their research report.

While Diageo (DEO) shares have underperformed the S&P 500 this year (-11% vs +30.5%, respectively), the stock has made steady improvement off the 4-year low set last month. Since then, Diageo has gained 8% which includes a 2.8% gain on Wednesday.

And analysts are mostly bullish on the stock, with Seeking Alpha authors and Wall Street analysts rating Diageo (DEO) a Buy. Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating views the stock as a Hold.

Among the top distillers and vintners stocks, Diageo (DEO) is ranked #4 behind Duckhorn Portfolio (NAPA), Constellations Brands (STZ), and Brown-Foreman (BF.A, BF.B).

data.title: TPG co-founder David Bonderman dies at 82 - report
data.description:
David Bonderman, the co-founder of private equity firm TPG (NASDAQ:TPG) who made his fortune through huge corporate takeovers, the rescue of struggling enterprises, and investments in startups, died on Wednesday at the age of 82, according to media reports.

In 1992, Bonderman and Jim Coulter, who he worked with at the Bass Family Office, made a bid to acquire Continental Airlines. The two formed TPG in the same year. The firm now has $239B in assets under management and has investments in more than 300 portfolio companies.

The company has developed a diverse set of investment strategies, including private equity, impact, credit, real estate, and market solutions, the company said on its website. TPG (NASDAQ:TPG) is now run by Jon Winkelried, who became sole CEO after serving as co-CEO since 2015.

data.title: Veeva dips after customer chooses Salesforce, Baird sees ramifications manageable
data.description:
Veeva Systems' (NYSE:VEEV) stock fell about 3% on Wednesday after an executive said that a customer chose Salesforce (CRM), which also drew reactions from analysts.

Veeva and Salesforce both develop customer relationship management, or CRM, software for business customers.

"We do expect to win the vast majority, but we certainly don't expect to win every customer. In fact, we were just recently made aware that one didn't go our way. They're going to go with -- they're going to do something custom with Salesforce," Paul Shawah, executive vice president of Strategy at Veeva, said at a Raymond James event.

Shawah added that it would be a very long-term project that will play-out over a period of time. "And this may not work out well for Salesforce. They don't have a product yet."

Shawah noted that they do not think that most large customers would take this sort of custom approach, and that the company is going to continue to win the vast majority of customers.

"We may have the opportunity to win this customer back. So we're focused on delivering a customer success and these kinds of things will happen," Shawah commented.

From a commitment perspective, Shawah said that the company just saw its fourth top 20 commitment, and was in discussions with the remaining top 20.

He added that migration decisions will be made mainly through the end of 2026.

Baird maintained its Outperform rating on Veeva.

The analysts said that Veeva continues to believe it will retain the vast majority of customers, and they think investors largely viewed this development as likely or inevitable.

The analysts added that they continue to believe the financial ramifications are manageable.

"While the term 'custom built' can have many interpretations, and we don't take an entity like Salesforce lightly, there is a history of customers opting to take this approach (most notably with IQVIA's OCE), where track record was ultimately mixed," said the analysts.

The analysts noted that this is expected to be a long-dated project, with Veeva's management noting that "a lot can happen between now and then" and that there could be potential to win this customer back in the future.
